lessons.title: Scenario
lessons.intro: You're scrolling TikTok and stop on this post.
lessons.sub_intro: It claims ashwagandha is guaranteed to boost your focus.

post.headline: KEY BENEFITS OF ASHWAGANDHA
post.highlight: Ashwagandha is GUARANTEED to improve your focus, attention, and clarity of mind!
post.body: Ashwagandha has been shown to improve focus, attention span, and clarity.

lessons.question: Before you buy, what should you check first?

lessons.title: Three Signals to Check

signals.name: Source Signal
signals.body: Is a specific journal or study linked, not just mentioned? A post that names 'a study' without a clickable DOI or link is giving you zero verifiable evidence.

signals.name: Language Signal
signals.body: Does the wording hedge ('may support', 'suggests') or overclaim ('proven', 'guaranteed', 'cures')? Science speaks in probabilities. Absolutes are a red flag.

signals.name: Poster Signal
signals.body: Who is posting? A credentialed researcher, a for-profit supplement brand, or an anonymous wellness account? Conflict of interest shapes what gets shared.

lessons.footnote: All three matter. A post can have one signal right and two signals wrong.

post.headline: KEY BENEFITS OF ASHWAGANDHA
post.highlight: Ashwagandha is GUARANTEED to improve your focus, attention, and clarity of mind
post.body: Ashwagandha has been shown to improve focus, attention span, and clarity.

annotations.title: No source mentioned
annotations.body: 'Has been shown' — ask yourself: by whom? Which study? Without a linked source, this is an opinion presented as fact.

annotations.title: What to look for instead
annotations.body: A clickable DOI link (e.g. doi.org/...) or a PubMed ID. Journal name + author names alone are not enough — they can be fabricated.

annotations.title: Also notice the language
annotations.body: 'GUARANTEED' is problematic language; no health supplement can guarantee outcomes. This is overclaiming.

lessons.title: Signal 2: Language + Source
lessons.subtitle: How do these two posts differ?

good.study_title: Acute and Repeated Ashwagandha Supplementation Improves Markers of Cognitive Function and Mood
good.quote: Research suggests ashwagandha may support focus in some adults.

bad.quote: Ashwagandha is GUARANTEED to improve your focus, attention, and clarity of mind!

post.headline: Nutrients. 2024 — Acute and Repeated Ashwagandha Supplementation… [doi link shown]
post.highlight: Research suggests ashwagandha may support focus in some adults.
post.body: Come to our Brooklyn location — or order online!

annotations.title: Has a real journal citation with DOI
annotations.body: The source here is actually verifiable.

annotations.title: Uses hedged language ('may support')
annotations.body: The wording matches how science actually talks.

annotations.title: Poster is a supplement company — direct financial interest
annotations.body: Even accurate citations can be used selectively by a biased source. Ask: does the poster have a financial stake in what you believe?

lessons.title: Recap: 3 Signals

signals.name: Source Signal
signals.body: A claim without a linked, verifiable study (DOI or clickable URL) is an opinion. Screenshots alone are not enough.

signals.name: Language Signal
signals.body: Hedged words ('may', 'suggests') = honest science. Absolute words ('proven', 'cures', 'guaranteed') = red flag. Conspiracy framing ('they don't want you to know') is an automatic flag.

signals.name: Poster Signal
signals.body: Who's posting matters. A supplement seller, an anonymous wellness account, or a user with no institutional affiliation all warrant skepticism — even when they cite real studies.

lessons.footnote: Next time a health post stops your scroll, run the 3 signals before you believe it, share it, or buy it.
